title: Grayscale vs. Color Images
concept: Grayscale images use a single brightness channel per pixel, while color images use three channels — and each option is chosen depending on what a task actually needs.

exercises.prompt: An old black-and-white photo and a modern color photo both show a street scene. What's the core storage difference?

options.text: The black-and-white photo stores one brightness number per pixel; the color one stores three
There is no real difference in how they're stored
The black-and-white photo uses more numbers per pixel

exercises.explain: Right! Grayscale needs just one channel; color needs three.

cards.title: Not every task needs color to work
cards.body: Doctors reading X-rays, old security cameras, and even some modern AI systems often skip color entirely and work in grayscale. That's not a downgrade — for a lot of tasks, color isn't needed to get the job done, and dropping it saves space and computation.

cards.title: One channel vs. three channels
cards.body: A grayscale image has a single channel per pixel — one number representing brightness, from black (0) to white (255), with grays in between. A color image stacks three channels (red, green, blue) per pixel. Same grid size, but color images carry three times the pixel data.

cards.title: Like AM radio vs. full stereo sound
cards.body: Grayscale is a bit like listening to a song in mono over an old AM radio — you get the melody and rhythm just fine, but not the full richness. Color is like stereo sound with separate left and right channels — more detail, more data, and sometimes more than you actually need.

cards.title: Why an X-ray machine doesn't bother with color
cards.body: An X-ray image only needs to show how dense different tissues and bones are — darker or lighter shades of gray. There's no 'color' information the X-ray beam captures in the first place, so storing it as grayscale is not just simpler, it's the honest representation of what was measured.

cards.title: Grayscale isn't just 'color with the color removed'
cards.body: It's tempting to think grayscale images are just color photos with the color stripped away for style. Sometimes that's true (a filter), but often grayscale is the native format — many sensors (X-ray, some security cameras, older scanners) only ever capture brightness and never had color data to begin with.

cards.title: Choosing grayscale or color for a task
cards.body: The right choice depends on what information actually matters.

left.text:
Task only depends on brightness/shape.
Examples: X-rays, old scans, text documents.
Smaller files, faster processing.

right.text:
Task depends on hue, not just brightness.
Examples: ripe vs. unripe fruit, traffic lights.
Larger files, more data to process.

exercises.prompt: How many channels does a typical grayscale image have per pixel?
exercises.explain: Correct. Grayscale images store just one brightness value per pixel.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Every grayscale image started out as a color photo that had its color removed.
exercises.explain: False. Many devices, like X-ray machines, capture only brightness and never had color data at all.

exercises.sentence: A task that needs to tell a red traffic light apart from a green one should use ___ images, not grayscale.
exercises.explain: Color is correct. Distinguishing red from green requires hue information that grayscale throws away.

exercises.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about when to use grayscale vs. color?

options.text: Use grayscale for a task that only needs shapes and brightness, like reading handwritten digits
Always use color, because more data is always better no matter the task
Always use grayscale, because color images are never useful

exercises.explain: Exactly. The right format depends on whether the task actually needs hue information.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a system to sort ripe (red) tomatoes from unripe (green) ones on a conveyor belt. Should they use grayscale or color images, and why?

options.text: Color — ripeness here is signaled by hue (red vs. green), which grayscale brightness alone can't reliably capture
Grayscale — color is never useful for sorting produce
It doesn't matter, grayscale and color always contain the same information
Grayscale — three channels would confuse the system

exercises.explain: Exactly. Since ripeness depends on hue rather than just brightness, color channels carry information grayscale would lose.
